title: Fill the tumbler

placement.where: replaces the 'liquids take the shape of their container' demonstration; students pour water into different tumblers and watch the water rise to take each container's exact shape, while discovering which materials can hold water

windowMethods: pour(idOrArray) — THE do-verb: pour water into container(s) to test if each holds water; id is 'glass'|'steel'|'plastic'|'clay'|'cloth'|'paper' or an array (extras queue).
fill(idOrArray) — alias of pour: pour water into container(s) to test if each holds water; id is 'glass'|'steel'|'plastic'|'clay'|'cloth'|'paper' or an array (extras queue).

studentControls.control: Tap a tumbler tile (glass, steel, plastic, clay, cloth, paper)
studentControls.effect: the jug tilts and pours; for a waterproof tumbler the water RISES and fills the exact silhouette of that container (proving the water takes the container's shape), then locks it green with ✓ and a one-line reason; an absorbent tumbler shakes red, leaks, and is marked ✗
toggles the pour/correct/wrong/completion Web Audio cues
clears all filled tumblers, the score and the celebration, and returns the bench to its starting state

answerKey.rule: A liquid has no fixed shape of its own — it takes the shape of whatever container holds it. Hard, sealed, non-absorbent tumblers (glass cup, steel can, plastic bottle, clay pot) hold the water so it takes their shape; absorbent or soft materials (cloth bag, paper cone) soak it up and let it leak through.

reasons.glass: Hard, smooth and non-absorbent — the water settles and takes the shape of the cup.
reasons.steel: Solid metal walls with no gaps — water fills the can and takes its straight-sided shape.
reasons.plastic: Waterproof walls — the water takes the bottle's curved shape and stays inside.
reasons.clay: A baked, sealed vessel — water fills the round pot and takes its rounded shape.
reasons.cloth: Cloth fibres absorb water and let it seep through — it cannot keep a water shape.
reasons.paper: Paper softens when wet — water soaks through and tears it apart.

answerKey.operatorModeNote: ai = focused DEMO: the pouring bench (jug + water-fill phenomenon), the outcome read-out and the result lists stay visible, but the tumbler tray / action bar is hidden and student taps are inert (the agent drives via pour()/highlight()); a 👩‍🏫 watch caption + chip show. student = full interactive controls. Default student.

additionalProps.instructionText: Pour water into each tumbler and watch the water take the shape of its container!

scientificFacts: A liquid has no shape of its own — it takes the shape of whatever container it is poured into.
Water fills a container from the bottom up and its top surface stays flat and level.
Hard, sealed, non-absorbent materials such as glass, steel, plastic and baked clay can hold water.
Soft or absorbent materials such as cloth and paper soak up water and let it leak through, so they cannot hold a liquid's shape.
